speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is November 26, 1995. This is an interview with Clara Posner. Her maiden name is Koltun. [BACKGROUND NOISE] My name is Jennifer Joyce. We're in Woodside, New York, in the United States. And the interview is in English. Today's date is November 26, 1995. My name is Jennifer Joyce, and this is an interview with Clara Posner. We're in Woodside, New York. Could you tell me your name and where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Clara Posner. I'm born in Poland, in the city of Luboml.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm born 1916, and November 10.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about Luboml? What it was like? What was Luboml like when you were growing up? Was it a city? A town?

speaker: interviewee
text: City.

speaker: interviewer
text: A city.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was a nice city, big city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe it a little bit more for me? What-- what kind of people lived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was Jewish people, and Ukrainian, and Polish people. Before, my mother used to tell me, I don't remember, my mother told me before it was Ukraine. But after the First World War in 1918 or 1919, the Poland occupied there. And that's why, since then, was Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what kinds of things did people do there to earn money?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was all kind of a people. There were people, working people, like carpenters, like shoemakers, like, you know, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Tailor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tailor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tailor, something, yeah. And there was a lot of stores. People was, it was a big, a square, I'd say, a-- like a bazaar, you know? A big one. And around and around was stores. It was everything was there. Everything. Whatever you wanted, it was in the stores. And the most was Jewish people got stores.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your family. What-- what were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Moshe. He was a businessman. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Since I remember, he used to, went to the villages. He got a horse and wagon. He used to go to the villages. And he bought-- you know, on the villages, the Polish people or the Ukrainian people, they used to go for huntings. They shot. And [INAUDIBLE] he used to buy the skins, all the skins, you know? And this, you know, he was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about your mother. What was her name? What was she like?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Hanna. She was-- I [? don't know was she-- ?] I-- people used to tell me that my mother was very nice, and was the nicest girl in the city, she tells me. But she was-- she was young. I don't remember. She told me, she used to learn dressmaking. But then, she got married when she was only 18 year old. And my father was 20 year old. And she stop doing the dressmaking. She, right away, she got children, you know? She was a housewife. That's her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your-- your brothers and sisters. How many kids were there in the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were six of us. Mine oldest brother-- you want to know the name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest brother was Suchar. He was only 12 year old when my father died. We were six. And the youngest was a year old when my father died. He was 12. He used to go in cheder. You know how this mean? But when my father die, my mother took him out. First of all, she couldn't pay. In the second, she gave him to no man.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother got a girlfriend. And in our city was, what you say? In Polish, they used to call it [POLISH]. They used to make vodka. And my mother's girlfriend, her husband was the manager. They was two Jewish people, but he was the manager. And she, my mother went to him, and she told him, said, I want you to take my boy to work. He said, what-- I can't. He's 12 year old. Even in Europe, they're not supposed to take before 16. He's 12 year old. But my mother said, but I have not-- I-- I-- I need something, to him, to make some. I have nothing to how to live with my six children?

speaker: interviewee
text: He took him. Because he was the manager, he could do everything what he want. He took him, and he gave him only to pour on the vodka in the bottles. That's all what he can do. But he gave him the full amount, what he gave the all workers.

speaker: interviewer
text: In--

speaker: interviewee
text: The full amount was 12 zloty. Not dollar. Zloty. Polish zlotys a week, 12. In 16 pounds of flour, to bake bread, in a liter. You know how much a liter, 2 and 1/2 quart, a liter vodka. That's the way they was paid for the workman. And my mother used to go every Thursday night, before, you know, and he took-- she took the-- the 16 pounds of flour. He said, he was only 12 year old, he was a little boy. He said, he wouldn't carry them. He broke the bones. My mother used to go and take the flour and take the vodka. And they came home. And my mother, right away, that was Thursday, and she used to right away prepare for Friday to bake bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was your youngest brother, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was mine older 12-year-old. Mine oldest.

speaker: interviewer
text: The oldest brother. OK. Tell me about your other siblings. His-- his name was, again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Suchar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Suchar.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your other siblings.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then after her was a sister. Gula. She was, I think, three years younger. I don't know. I think she was-- about three younger. I think so. You think I remember everything. It's not yesterday. And then I came. I was born in 1916. And after me was three little brothers. One was Berel. [NON-ENGLISH] they used to call him. And the next was [? Sandy. ?] [? Sandy. ?] And the little one was a year old. Usha. Usha. Yeah. A year when my father died. This I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when did your father die?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a year, my father died a year before. I think my father died in 1923. I think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- what was the cause of his death?

speaker: interviewee
text: He got pneumonia. In that time, wasn't, you know, in Europe. It was not antibiotic, it wasn't-- think like this. He died. He was 30--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was 38 year old.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was 38. How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I was, that time, was seven year old. I don't remember exactly. Something like this. I just started to school, go after he was there. In Europe, we started, was seven years. I remember. I think so. I don't remember. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about your-- your family life. You said you started school when you were seven?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- where did you go? What kind of school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was a Polish school already. Polish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The school. Polish school. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you study there? How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- there what I started, they used to call it Talmud Torah. Maybe you understand what I mean? There, we was only Jewish children there, until fifth-- fifth grades. If I want to go to finish seven grades, only until seven, that's all. Then I'm supposed to leave the Talmud Torah and go [INAUDIBLE]-- a different street that was only Polish children. Yeah, I went there for two years. Yeah. [INAUDIBLE] they used to call us [NON-ENGLISH]. Like here you finished the high school. That's all. No more.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kinds of things did you study there, in both schools?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything-- everything like you studied here. Same thing. Polish, math.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have-- you had religious instruction, also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only when I was in Talmud Torah. There was a Jewish teacher. He used to-- yeah, he used to. I remember it was-- the name was Itzhak Shapira. I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the name of your teacher?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Itzhak Shapira. I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember when he was, came, and he introduced himself. He was a Jewish boy. He was, you know, very religious. Itzhak Shapira. And then he got married with a teacher, a Jewish teacher. And that's all. That's I remember then. After this, I don't remember. I-- I-- when I left the Talmud Torah, and I went to the Polish school, he wasn't there. He was only the-- in the Jewish, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about synagogue? Did your family go to a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me anything that you remember about the synagogue? The name? Or what did it look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a lot-- there was not only one. Was a few. Was a big one. I think I got a picture. A big [? shul. ?] And there was all kind of was. It was to whom you belong. Like Hasidim, like theirs was a [NON-ENGLISH] synagogue. It was original, it was [NON-ENGLISH], it was [NON-ENGLISH]. [INAUDIBLE] It was even, you know, all of schusters and schneiders, you understand what I mean? All of theirs was shoemakers and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tailors.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tailors.

speaker: interviewee
text: Schneiders. How you call this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tailors.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tailors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tailors. That's all. Only for them. They got upset, but I don't know why.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: They was fine people. They were fine people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't know what they went in a different synagogue. That's what I remember. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the synagogue that your family went to, what-- what was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Synagogue [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it-- was it orthodox or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Orthodox. Yeah. My mother used to go. My father, you know, every Shabbos. My father [INAUDIBLE] used to go. But my mother used to go every Shabbos, I remember. And she used to put on a wig. Not everybody did. But she did, because she was very reli-- she told me, when she got married, she was only 18 year old, and they cut it. The religious people cut the hair and put on a wig. She was 18 year old, and she said, you know something? I was right away 20 years older with a wig. [LAUGHS] You know? Now, when I got married, my sister, she said, now it is good. You're not supposed to cut a hair. You're not supposed to wear a wig. You go like you want it. She always used to tell us. She used to say, I was a beautiful-- I remember, a neighbor told me once, to me, Chaya. My name, the Jewish name is Chaya. She used to say, you know, Chaya, [INAUDIBLE] at that time, 10 year old, or 12, I don't remember. She said, you a little bit like your mother, but not-- your mother was more nice. [LAUGHS] She says, you only a little bit like your mother. Not like your mother. Your mother was the nicest girl in the city. [LAUGHS] She was only 53 when Hitler killed her.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said your mother was-- was very religious.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same thing. For the men, never mind. The men used to daven, pray, every day. If they didn't went to the synagogue, they used to daven in home, with the [NON-ENGLISH]. But the ladies, not everybody from the ladies went to the synagogue Shabbos. But my mother went.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember your mother going.

speaker: interviewee
text: So do I. So do I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family keep kosher?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, come on. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I remember my mother, for Passover, in the table, she-- she made [NON-ENGLISH] a special, a cover, a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tablecloth.

speaker: interviewee
text: A [NON-ENGLISH]. A cover, you know? On the-- on the table. She used to scrape the table, and wash and this. And then, after this, she put a, you know, like a what you call it? A cover, you know? And I used to say, Mama, you washed the table and everything, and what you need this? I say, don't ask me questions. You're too little to ask me. You don't know nothing. And then she used to have, every Passover, a tablecloth, she didn't use for all year, only for Passover. And even the chairs, she used to cover with this. I said, Mama, we only sit at the chair. We don't eat. Don't ask me questions. Sometime, you kids, you put on a piece of matzoh, piece of this. Supposed to be covered. This was my Mama. This is, she got glasses only for dairy and for non-dairy. [LAUGHS] [INAUDIBLE] it's a lot to talk about this. This, [INAUDIBLE] not only my mama, my grandmother and every-- the whole family was like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any holidays that you remember in particular, where the whole family was there? Can you tell me about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like here, same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Tell me about it. Who was there? Where did you usually go for the holidays, for Passover?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother used to go, but not, we were children. But I remember, I used to go with my sister on Rosh Hashanah, you know what it means? To hear the shofar [? blow ?] on Rosh Hashanah. All the girls, we used to go to the big shul, and there was a special room, where we can go in, only ladies and girls. And we used to go to hear shofar [? blow. ?] You know what I mean? Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, when my father died, my sister and I, we used to go to say, what we say? How you call it? Yizkor. You know what I mean? We used to go for Yizkor every year. Yeah. In the same, the big shul, there was a place where they let in girls, ladies. You know? Yeah. So when you were growing in Luboml, did you ever feel that you were different because you were Jewish? Was there any problem being Jewish? When you were growing up.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not from my time. Not from my time. I wouldn't say. There was plenty [? anti-Semitism. ?] But they didn't show, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how was there anti-Semitism? You said it wasn't-- it wasn't obvious.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was.

speaker: interviewer
text: It wasn't obvious.

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we didn't know before. That was good. The Polish people, the Ukrainian. You know? But we realized then, when the war started. Then we realized. Then we saw what they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you were going to school, as a young girl, were there any problems?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, I went five years only with Jewish children. And then, when I went to the Polish school, oh, yeah. Oh, yeah. The boys, yeah. The girls, no. The Polish girls, no. But the boys, you know, a punch here, a punch-- you know? But not, not very much. A little bit, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about some of that, what you remember specifically?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: The boys in the Polish school. They would give you a hard time because you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: They did a little. Not much. They just afraid, they was afraid, for the teachers. The teachers, if there was something, a boy, you know, when we going, and use to say this-- we fall, you know what I mean? But when we went to the teacher, told them, the teachers said-- [INAUDIBLE]. The teachers was all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what about the other kids? Do you remember anybody ever doing anything to you? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no. Not-- not special. No, no. They was afraid. There was a principal. There was teacher. They was afraid, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did things start to seem like they were changing? When did things start to change?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war started, in 1939. They right away was-- not much of the Polish. There was Ukrainian police. They started right away. They was next to Hitler, next to the SS. Yeah. Only the Ukrainian. The Polish, maybe there was. I don't know. But I know the Ukrainian. Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they like? Where did you see them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw them every day. I saw them. They-- they-- they made a ghetto. The ghetto start. They came in, and-- first of all, wait a minute. I'll tell you. First came, if you remember, if you know, I think everybody knows, that Hitler made a pact with the Russian. I think you know. Everybody knows. That Ukraine would be for the Russian, and the rest, Poland, the rest for Hitler, for the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: They came to us, I remember, in June, I think. I can't remember exactly. I think was in June. And they told us, the German told us, that they're going to stay only five days. And they going to leave to Poland. Poland was-- the next city from Luboml was Chelm. You know this? Just an hour by train to go from our city to Chelm was just one hour by train. And they were occupied by the German.

speaker: interviewee
text: And our city was the last city for the-- for the Ukrainian. And they told us, the German, they, we're going to be here only five days, and then we leave. And the Russian will come. And that's the way it was. The Russian came after five days. And they was in our city, the Russian, a year.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then, all of a sudden, I remembered, it was on a Saturday night. And we hear, like, we talked-- I think it was thundering, or something. And I said, my God, it was a beautiful day. How come all of a sudden? Then, after another, another, another, and then, the neighbors, we came outside. And it wasn't thundering. It was the German. The German-- how you say? Start on the Russian, you know? They attack the Russian. The Russian didn't-- they didn't know. They didn't expect it. And that's the way start the war. Right before the-- the war started, can you tell me what was going on in your-- with your family? Were you-- were you still in school? Were you working?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. When I finished school, the seven grades, I went-- I went to learn for dressmaking. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a-- a-- a Jewish lady. I went to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rachel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I know. [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH]. She's in Israel. She's in Israel. She left before the war. Yeah. I know. She's in--

speaker: interviewer
text: So how-- how old were you when you went to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: After when I finished the seven grades, I went to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you worked with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I learned. She learned. She taught me how to do, yeah. My mother told me I had to go to learn. I got to help, to give us, to help something in house. I give my mama. Whatever we made, I give my mama a few zlotys.

speaker: interviewer
text: She needed. It was six children. She didn't work. What can I say? She suffered plenty.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember lots of times, a lots of time. I remember one night, she didn't get supper for us. She didn't get. And then, we been sitting, she says, you know, I'm going outside. I'll be right back. She went. I don't know where she went. I don't know what she did. And she came home with the bread and a herring, and she made coffee-- we didn't got coffee, no, no, no. Chicory. And she made us supper, and that's all. There was a lot of times she didn't got what to get with the food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that after your father died, things became more difficult?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's was after my father died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: When my father was alive, oh, when my father was alive, I remember we got a maid.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Devoirah. ?] Dora. Here we'd call her Dora. [? Devoirah. ?] She was across the street. She was come-- she came every day to help my mother with the children, and that. But she went-- she went home every night to sleep. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about her? What did she look like? What else do you remember about her, the maid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. I remember she was a neighbor, that's all. She was, that time, maybe 20 year old, or something. I don't know. I remember. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after your father died, you said that your oldest brother went to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: Went to work there. But I told you where he went to. Yeah. He was there. He was there, the whole, the father to her, until Hitler killed him. He was her father. He gave everything. My mother, he used to work. No, he didn't work all the time there. Then he work at different places. Whatever he made, he gave to Mama. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your other brothers and sisters? They were in school? Or?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. The younger. Yeah, the younger went to the-- to the same school like I went, the same thing, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So later on, after you learned the dressmaking, where did you work? In a store? Where did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where I work?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: A few years, I work there, by the lady. And then, my brother, the older brother, gave me-- I don't remember how much, 25-- that wasn't dollars, zlotys or something. He said, you know, I'll buy you a sewing machine. I know you know something. And my mother told me lots of stuff. My mother know I think [INAUDIBLE]-- she was a dressmaker before she got married. And he bought me a sewing machine. And I started to my house, until the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were-- you were working out of your home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Starting when, and when did you stop doing that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember when. I can't exactly remember. I was, that time, maybe 18 year old or something. I don't remember exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did people come to you? How did-- how did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I told them. My mother told them. I told them. And they start to come into, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: All kinds of people?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, the neighbors. The neighbors, yeah. Neighbors. Yeah. Whatever I-- I sew, I said, whether I-- I do mine sewing, and, you know, they came. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- when the war started, did things start to-- how did it affect you personally? How did things start to change with your work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. When the war started-- first of all, I got married. I got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: A year before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: To who? The name was-- I got a picture. The name was Lebel. Yeah. Yeah. And I got a baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lebel? What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Horen. H-O-R-E-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what can you tell me about your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. What's to say? He was--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- how did you meet him?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know how we meet, boys and girls. And that's all. And then the war started. And then, they made a ghetto. Not right away-- took a few months.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was the first memory that you have, after the war, of-- of things changing? How did you know that things were changing? What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean, after the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: Once the war started, things changed.

speaker: interviewee
text: The shul?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. What is the first-- what were the first signs?

speaker: interviewee
text: They start when they came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: They--

speaker: interviewee
text: The German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they came the first day. They chase out a lot of-- can't tell you, half a city, they chase out from the house, and they, for-- they pour on gasoline, and [NON-ENGLISH]. And they burn all the houses. Houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans came in and they did-- they poured gasoline?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] I don't know how they-- [NON-ENGLISH] all the houses with I don't know what. Gasoline, or something. And [NON-ENGLISH]. They burn the houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: They burned-- they burned the houses.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, half a city. And then the people, were they chasing over, they came. They didn't get anywhere to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in your-- in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when did this happen? Do you remember when it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first day when the German came. The first day when they attacked the Russian. The first day there was only five days. But then the next time, when they came and they attack the Russian, that started. That started.
